name: Suzutani
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima (Hyogo). Sire Tanishige FB211; dam Suzunami FB227 (by Shigeshigenami FB219); maternal granddam Suzuko (by Yasuchiyo Doi). With Rikitani, one of the first 100% Tajima females ever to leave Japan.
notable_offspring: Dam of the two most important 2nd-generation sires outside Japan — Shigeshigetani and Sanjirou — plus Kanadagene 100D, Suzuharu FB5769 and Suzumichi FB13047. The Shigeshigetani sire line descends from her.
marbling_note: Regarded as the greatest Tajima and most influential female ever exported; a very prolific flush cow whose Tajima genetics are a cornerstone of marbling quality in fullblood herds worldwide.
bio: Suzutani is widely called the most famous and genetically influential Wagyu cow ever to leave Japan. A 100% Tajima female born December 28, 1991, she was sired by Tanishige (FB211) and out of Suzunami (FB227), a daughter of the legendary Tajima sire Shigeshigenami. In 1993 the Mannett Group (which became World K's) imported her alongside Rikitani and Okutani plus the bulls Michifuku and Haruki II — the shipment that founded fullblood Wagyu in North America. Unlike her less fertile shipmate Rikitani, Suzutani proved an exceptionally prolific flush cow. She produced Shigeshigetani and Sanjirou, arguably the two most consequential second-generation sires bred outside Japan, and the Shigeshigetani line traces directly to her. Additional offspring extended her reach into Canada. Because so many elite modern fullbloods carry her blood, she remains a benchmark maternal name in Wagyu pedigrees and a symbol of concentrated Tajima marbling genetics.
photo_note: Foundation cows are rarely photographed; no verified image located.
notes: Registration FB1617 and pedigree confirmed via WSU Foundation Wagyu Parentage document (Rocking 711) and Wagyu World.

name: Rikitani
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima. Sire Tanishige FB211; dam Shigekane FB230 (by Shigeshigenami); maternal granddam Nishino 179. With Suzutani, among the first 100% Tajima females exported.
notable_offspring: Son Rikihari/Rikiharu (FB10973) was the first fullblood Wagyu calf born outside Japan (June 19, 1994, Canada), sired by Haruki II.
marbling_note: Not a prolific flush cow and left few progeny, but her descendants are noted for exceptional marbling scores.
bio: Rikitani was one of the three near-100% Tajima foundation females imported by the Mannett Group in 1993, the shipment that established fullblood Wagyu in North America. Born January 13, 1992, she was a pure Tajima female sired by Tanishige (FB211) and out of Shigekane (FB230), a daughter of the great Tajima sire Shigeshigenami — the shared ancestor that made all three 1993 heifers so prized. Together with Suzutani she was among the very first 100% Tajima females ever to leave Japan. Her enduring place in history comes from her son Rikihari (also rendered Rikiharu, FB10973), sired by Haruki II, who on June 19, 1994 in Canada became the first fullblood Wagyu calf born anywhere outside Japan. Rikitani herself proved a poor flush cow and produced comparatively few offspring, so her direct genetic footprint is narrower than Suzutani's or Okutani's, but her progeny are prized for elite marbling and her name anchors the earliest chapter of the breed's export history.
notes: FB1618 and pedigree from WSU Parentage document and Wagyu World; calf name appears as both Rikihari and Rikiharu (FB10973).

name: Okutani
bloodline_detail: ~75% Tajima / 25% Fujiyoshi (Shimane). Sire Okushige FB218 (No.1 for BMS in Japan 1990); dam Fujitani FB223 (by Kikutani 908); maternal granddam Itofuji 9 (by Dai 7 Itozakura). Considered the strongest sire stack of the 1993 trio.
notable_offspring: Embryo dam of Fujiko (FB1984) — the first fullblood Wagyu born in the USA (June 24, 1994, by Haruki II) — plus Beijiro, Genjiro and Okuharu. Fujiko's cloning rights sold for ~$25,000 in 2012.
marbling_note: Carried an elite concentration of top BMS sires (Okushige, Kikutani, Dai 7 Itozakura, Shigeshigenami); regarded as equally influential to Suzutani despite her early death.
bio: Okutani was the third of the Mannett Group's landmark 1993 foundation females and, on pedigree, arguably the most powerfully bred of the trio. Born January 5, 1992, she was roughly 75% Tajima and 25% Fujiyoshi (Shimane), sired by Okushige (FB218) — the bull ranked No. 1 for marbling in Japan in 1990 — and out of Fujitani (FB223), a daughter of the elite sire Kikutani 908, with Dai 7 Itozakura appearing through her granddam. This stacked her pedigree with several of the greatest marbling sires in Japanese history. Tragically she died soon after arriving in the United States, so few live progeny exist, but embryos recovered from her changed the breed: on June 24, 1994 she produced Fujiko (FB1984), the first fullblood Wagyu ever born in the USA, sired by Haruki II. Fujiko became one of the breed's most influential and defect-free females, her cloning rights fetching about $25,000 in 2012. Through Fujiko, Okutani's genetics remain foundational across American fullblood herds.
notes: FB1616, birth date and pedigree from Wagyu World; bloodline split and offspring cross-checked with Reserve Cattle Co. and Rocking 711.

name: Okahana
bloodline_detail: Precise strain composition not documented. Part of the Mannett Group's second (1994) black shipment.
notable_offspring: Founder of the 'Okahana' maternal line still cited in modern pedigrees.
bio: Okahana was one of four black foundation females — with Nakayuki, Kanetani and Nakagishi 5 — imported from Japan by the Mannett Group (World K's) in August 1994, a shipment that also brought the bulls Kenhanafuji and Takazakura. This second consignment broadened the maternal base of the young North American fullblood herd beyond the three celebrated 1993 cows. Calves registered from the group after arrival included Tanitsuru, Nakazakura, Kitaguni Jr and Reiko. Okahana's specific pedigree and registration number are not well documented in public breeder sources, but her name survives as a recognized maternal line in later pedigrees. She matters as part of the small nucleus of genuinely Japan-exported foundation females on which the entire fullblood population outside Japan was built.
notes: Import event well documented; registration, birth year, sire and dam not found — left null.

name: Nakayuki
bloodline_detail: Strain composition not documented. Part of the Mannett Group's second (1994) black shipment.
bio: Nakayuki was one of the four black foundation females imported from Japan by the Mannett Group (World K's) in August 1994, alongside Okahana, Kanetani and Nakagishi 5 and the bulls Kenhanafuji and Takazakura. This shipment supplemented the famous 1993 trio of Suzutani, Rikitani and Okutani and helped widen the female genetic base of the fledgling North American fullblood herd. Post-arrival calves from the group included Tanitsuru, Nakazakura, Kitaguni Jr and Reiko. Detailed pedigree, registration and birth data for Nakayuki are not published in the breeder sources reviewed. She is significant as one of the limited number of verifiable Japan-exported foundation dams behind today's fullblood cattle.
notes: Import event attested; individual pedigree/registration not found — left null.

name: Kanetani
bloodline_detail: Strain composition not documented. Part of the Mannett Group's second (1994) black shipment.
bio: Kanetani was one of four black foundation females the Mannett Group (World K's) imported from Japan in August 1994, together with Okahana, Nakayuki and Nakagishi 5 and the bulls Kenhanafuji and Takazakura. Arriving a year after the celebrated Suzutani/Rikitani/Okutani trio, this group expanded the maternal foundation of the early North American fullblood herd, producing registered calves such as Tanitsuru, Nakazakura, Kitaguni Jr and Reiko. Public breeder records give little individual detail on Kanetani's pedigree, registration or birth year. Her importance lies in her membership of the small, closed set of authentic Japan-exported foundation females underpinning the breed abroad.
notes: Import event attested; individual pedigree/registration not found — left null.

name: Nakagishi 5
bloodline_detail: Pedigree reported to trace to Dai 7 (Dai Nana) Itozakura and to Harumi on both sire and dam sides — strong Itozakura/Fujiyoshi (Shimane) influence rather than pure Tajima.
marbling_note: Valued for the Itozakura influence in her pedigree, a line associated with frame and size as well as marbling.
bio: Nakagishi 5 was one of the four black foundation females imported from Japan by the Mannett Group (World K's) in August 1994, alongside Okahana, Nakayuki and Kanetani plus the bulls Kenhanafuji and Takazakura. She is the best-characterized of that group's dams: her pedigree is reported to carry the great sire Dai 7 Itozakura together with Harumi appearing on both the sire and dam sides, giving her a distinctly Itozakura/Shimane-influenced makeup rather than the pure Tajima of the 1993 cows. This made her useful for adding line diversity to a narrow founding gene pool. Registered calves from the 1994 group after arrival included Tanitsuru, Nakazakura, Kitaguni Jr and Reiko. Her exact registration number and birth year were not found in public sources. She matters as a verifiable Japan-exported foundation dam contributing non-Tajima strength to the breed's maternal base outside Japan.
notes: Dai 7 Itozakura/Harumi pedigree note from a single breeder-summary source.

name: Namiko
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto Prefecture. One of the nine red foundation females in the 1994 Wood importation and one of the seven purchased for HeartBrand.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood (with Yukio Kurosawatsu) → HeartBrand Ranch
marbling_note: Akaushi are prized for healthy monounsaturated (oleic) fat; the HeartBrand foundation dams are the genetic base of essentially all registered fullblood Akaushi outside Japan.
bio: Namiko was one of nine red (Akaushi) foundation females in the pivotal 1994 importation led by Dr. Al and Marie Wood, working with Mr. Yukio Kurosawatsu out of Kumamoto Prefecture — the shipment that created the entire fullblood Akaushi population outside Japan. She was among the seven females (with Akiko, Haruko, Fuyuko, Ume, Dai 8 Marunami and Ringo 117) subsequently acquired for HeartBrand Ranch in Harwood, Texas, alongside the bulls Shigemaru, Tamamaru and Hikari. The selection was made in Japan by Bruce Hemingson, who chose the foundation cattle firsthand; the first fullblood Akaushi born on American soil, Big Al, came from her shipmate Akiko, bred to Dai 10 Mitsumaru in quarantine. Individual pedigree detail for Namiko is not published in the sources reviewed. Her significance is collective: as one of a tiny closed founder set, she is an irreplaceable pillar of the Akaushi breed abroad, whose descendants are registered with both the American Wagyu Association and the Akaushi Association of America.
notes: Import event, Kumamoto origin and HeartBrand routing well attested; individual pedigree/registration not found.

name: Akiko
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. One of the nine 1994 Wood red foundation females; one of the seven HeartBrand founders. Distinct from the US-born BLACK cow 'Akiko' FB9499 (a Suzutani daughter).
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood → HeartBrand Ranch
notable_offspring: Dam of Big Al (registered Mitsuaki), sired by Dai 10 Mitsumaru — conceived in Japan's quarantine and born in America as the first fullblood Akaushi on American soil.
marbling_note: Foundation of the HeartBrand Akaushi program known for high oleic-acid, health-oriented marbling.
bio: Akiko is the Akaushi cow behind one of the great origin stories of American Wagyu — an account preserved firsthand by Bruce Hemingson, who selected the foundation cattle in Japan. One of the best red females chosen for Dr. Al Wood (a heart surgeon) and his wife Marie, she came up open — not pregnant — during the roughly six-month export quarantine every animal had to pass in Japan. In a founding group of only a handful of cows, losing a season from one of the best was unthinkable. But getting her bred was nearly impossible: her sire line and the elite Akaushi genetics traced to Kumamoto on the island of Kyushu, and cattle and semen could not simply be moved across Japan's prefecture lines. So, by Hemingson's account, they arranged to meet a man in a grocery-store parking lot in Japan and paid $5,000 cash for a single straw of the elite sire Dai 10 Mitsumaru. Then they slipped into the quarantine facility after dark; with no chute available, they caught Akiko and held her pinned against the wall while a vet artificially inseminated her by lantern-light. It worked. Akiko arrived in America pregnant and gave birth here to Big Al (registered Mitsuaki) — the first fullblood Akaushi born on American soil and a cornerstone sire of the HeartBrand program. She should not be confused with the later US-born black cow 'Akiko' (FB9499), a Suzutani daughter; the two share only a name. Few foundation females carry a legacy as vivid, or as consequential, as hers.
notes: Big Al story per Bruce Hemingson (firsthand participant). CORRECTED: bred in Japan's quarantine, not the US — Akiko arrived pregnant and calved in America. Sire of Big Al = Dai 10 Mitsumaru. Distinct from black cow Akiko FB9499.

name: Haruko
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. One of the nine 1994 Wood red foundation females; one of the seven HeartBrand founders.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood → HeartBrand Ranch
marbling_note: Part of the closed founder set behind HeartBrand/registered Akaushi and its high monounsaturated-fat reputation.
bio: Haruko was one of nine red Akaushi foundation females imported from Kumamoto Prefecture in 1994 by Dr. Al and Marie Wood, and one of the seven subsequently purchased to establish HeartBrand Ranch in Harwood, Texas, together with Namiko, Akiko, Fuyuko, Ume, Dai 8 Marunami and Ringo 117 and the bulls Shigemaru, Tamamaru and Hikari. This single importation is the genetic wellspring of virtually all fullblood Akaushi outside Japan. The heifers were bred to elite Japanese sires Namimaru and Dai 10 Mitsumaru before export. Haruko's specific pedigree is not documented in the public sources reviewed. She matters as one of only a handful of authentic red foundation dams from which the entire Akaushi maternal base abroad descends.
notes: Founder status well attested; individual pedigree/registration not found.

name: Fuyuko
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. One of the nine 1994 Wood red foundation females; one of the seven HeartBrand founders.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood → HeartBrand Ranch
marbling_note: Founder of the HeartBrand Akaushi maternal base; Akaushi valued for oleic-rich, health-forward fat.
bio: Fuyuko was one of the nine red Akaushi foundation females in Dr. Al and Marie Wood's 1994 importation from Kumamoto Prefecture and one of the seven acquired to found HeartBrand Ranch in Harwood, Texas. Her cohort — bred before export to the top Japanese sires Namimaru and Dai 10 Mitsumaru — represents the complete and closed founding gene pool of fullblood Akaushi outside Japan, now registered through both the American Wagyu Association and the Akaushi Association of America. Individual pedigree records for Fuyuko were not found in public sources. She is significant as one of the small nucleus of genuine red foundation dams behind every registered Akaushi abroad.
notes: Founder status well attested; individual pedigree/registration not found.

name: Ume
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. One of the nine 1994 Wood red foundation females; one of the seven HeartBrand founders.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood → HeartBrand Ranch
marbling_note: Part of the HeartBrand founder set underpinning registered Akaushi genetics.
bio: Ume was one of the nine red Akaushi foundation females imported from Kumamoto Prefecture in 1994 by Dr. Al and Marie Wood, and one of the seven retained to establish HeartBrand Ranch in Harwood, Texas, alongside Namiko, Akiko, Haruko, Fuyuko, Dai 8 Marunami and Ringo 117. The importation — with the bulls Shigemaru, Tamamaru and Hikari — is the origin point of fullblood Akaushi outside Japan. The females were bred to the leading Japanese Akaushi sires Namimaru and Dai 10 Mitsumaru before shipment. Ume's individual pedigree is not documented in the sources reviewed. As one of the tiny closed founder group, she is an essential maternal pillar of the Akaushi breed abroad.
notes: Founder status well attested; individual pedigree/registration not found.

name: Namoi
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. One of the nine 1994 Wood red foundation females. Routed to Canada (Wagyu Sekai) rather than HeartBrand.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood (import); acquired by Wagyu Sekai (Canada)
notable_offspring: Taken to Canada with her calf; part of the Canadian Akaushi foundation.
bio: Namoi — also rendered Naomi — was one of nine red Akaushi foundation females in Dr. Al and Marie Wood's 1994 importation from Kumamoto Prefecture. Rather than joining the HeartBrand nucleus in Texas, she and her calf were taken to Canada by Wagyu Sekai (Yukio and Ken Kurosawatsu), together with Dai 3 Namiaki, helping seed the Canadian Akaushi foundation. Her specific pedigree is not documented in the sources reviewed. She matters as one of the very small set of authentic red foundation dams exported before the 1997 Japanese ban, and as a bridge of Akaushi genetics into Canada.
notes: Spelled Namoi and Naomi across sources. Canadian routing via Wagyu Sekai from Akaushi Insight.

name: Dai 3 Namiaki
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. One of the nine 1994 Wood red foundation females. Routed to Canada (Wagyu Sekai).
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood (import); acquired by Wagyu Sekai (Canada)
notable_offspring: Taken to Canada with her calf.
bio: Dai 3 Namiaki (appearing in some records as Dai 3 Namiaki Ni) was one of the nine red Akaushi foundation females imported from Kumamoto Prefecture in 1994 by Dr. Al and Marie Wood. Along with Namoi, she and her calf were taken to Canada by Wagyu Sekai following the importation, contributing to the Canadian Akaushi foundation rather than the Texas HeartBrand herd. Her individual pedigree is not published in the sources reviewed. Her 'Dai 3' prefix reflects Japanese Akaushi naming convention. She counts among the limited, closed group of genuine red foundation dams that predate the 1997 export ban.
notes: Canadian routing from Akaushi Insight; name variant Dai 3 Namiaki Ni noted.

name: Dai 9 Koubai 73
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. One of the nine 1994 Wood red foundation females. Purchased from Mr. Yukio Kurosawatsu.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood (from Yukio Kurosawatsu)
bio: Dai 9 Koubai 73 (also written Dai 9 Kobai) was one of the nine red Akaushi foundation females in Dr. Al and Marie Wood's 1994 importation from Kumamoto Prefecture. She was specifically purchased from Mr. Yukio Kurosawatsu, the Japanese collaborator on the import. Her pedigree is not documented in the public sources reviewed. As one of a very small, closed set of authentic Japanese Red foundation females exported before the 1997 ban, she forms part of the maternal base of registered Akaushi outside Japan.
notes: Purchase-source detail from Akaushi Insight; spelling varies Koubai/Kobai.

name: Dai 8 Marunami
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. One of the nine 1994 Wood red foundation females; among the seven acquired for HeartBrand.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood → HeartBrand Ranch
marbling_note: Named foundation dam frequently cited in HeartBrand/Akaushi pedigrees.
bio: Dai 8 Marunami was one of the nine red Akaushi foundation females imported from Kumamoto Prefecture in 1994 by Dr. Al and Marie Wood, and is specifically listed among the seven females that became the HeartBrand Ranch nucleus in Harwood, Texas. Her 'Dai 8' prefix follows Japanese Akaushi naming, and the Marunami family is a recognized Akaushi cow line. Detailed pedigree data for her was not found in the public sources reviewed. She matters as one of the small closed group of genuine red foundation dams whose genetics underpin essentially all registered fullblood Akaushi outside Japan.
notes: Named among the seven HeartBrand founders (Akaushi Insight); individual pedigree not found.

name: Himawari 245
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi. Purchased from Takeda (Japan) as part of the broader mid-1990s Akaushi importation; USDA-documented per breeder history.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood / HeartBrand (from Takeda)
bio: Himawari 245 was an additional red Akaushi foundation female acquired in the mid-1990s HeartBrand importation, purchased from Takeda in Japan (a transaction breeder histories note as USDA-documented) rather than through the core nine-head Wood shipment. Together with Hitomi 244 she extended the imported Akaushi female base beyond the primary Kumamoto group. Her pedigree is not published in the sources reviewed. She is included as a verifiable named red foundation female contributing to the Akaushi maternal foundation abroad.
notes: Named in Akaushi Insight as purchased from Takeda; import year assumed 1994, not explicitly dated in source.

name: Hitomi 244
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi. Purchased from Takeda (Japan); USDA-documented per breeder history.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood / HeartBrand (from Takeda)
bio: Hitomi 244 was an additional red Akaushi foundation female purchased from Takeda in Japan as part of the mid-1990s HeartBrand importation, alongside Himawari 245. Like the other foundation Akaushi dams, she originates from the Japanese Red population centered in Kumamoto Prefecture. Her detailed pedigree is not documented in the public sources reviewed. She is included as a verifiable named red foundation female that broadened the imported Akaushi maternal base outside Japan.
notes: Named in Akaushi Insight as purchased from Takeda; import year not explicitly stated.

name: Ringo 117
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. Born in Japan in 1994 as the natural calf of foundation dam Dai 8 Marunami, and imported with her in the Wood shipment.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood → HeartBrand Ranch
bio: Ringo 117 was born in Japan in 1994, the natural calf of the foundation Akaushi dam Dai 8 Marunami, and came to America with her in Dr. Al and Marie Wood's Kumamoto importation — one of the nine females and three bulls that founded HeartBrand and the entire fullblood Akaushi population outside Japan. As a young imported female she is counted among the HeartBrand foundation dams. (An earlier ambiguity in some accounts — whether she was imported or first-generation US-born — is resolved by her documented 1994 Japanese birth to Dai 8 Marunami.) Further individual pedigree detail is not published in the sources reviewed, but her place among the small closed group of genuine Akaushi founders is secure.
notes: Resolved: Ringo 117 born Japan 1994, natural calf of Dai 8 Marunami, imported with her dam (per Beyond Beef / Akaushi Genetics).

name: Taguchi 9
bloodline_detail: Part of the Mannett Group's 1997 black female import (seven females + bull Yasufuku Jr). Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Taguchi 9 was one of seven black foundation females imported from Japan by the Mannett Group (World K's) in 1997 — the group's final import, arriving with the bull Yasufuku Jr just as Japan closed live cattle exports. This late shipment (with Nakahana 5, Mitsutaka, Okuito 9, Hanateru 9, Rabito and Hisako) added fresh female lines to the North American fullblood herd right at the end of the export window. Individual pedigree detail for Taguchi 9 is not published in the sources reviewed. She matters as one of the last verifiable Japan-exported foundation females before the 1997 ban.
notes: Named in the 1997 Mannett import list (Rocking 711, Wagyu International); no individual pedigree found.

name: Nakahana 5
bloodline_detail: Part of the Mannett Group's 1997 black female import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Nakahana 5 was one of seven black foundation females in the Mannett Group's 1997 import from Japan, which also brought the bull Yasufuku Jr. Arriving at the very end of the pre-ban export era alongside Taguchi 9, Mitsutaka, Okuito 9, Hanateru 9, Rabito and Hisako, she helped broaden the maternal base of the fullblood herd outside Japan. Her individual pedigree was not found in the sources reviewed. She is included as a verifiable named Japan-exported foundation female from the final Mannett consignment.
notes: Named in 1997 Mannett import list; no individual pedigree found.

name: Mitsutaka
bloodline_detail: Part of the Mannett Group's 1997 black female import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Mitsutaka was one of the seven black foundation females imported from Japan by the Mannett Group in 1997, the final World K's consignment that also included the bull Yasufuku Jr. Her cohort arrived just before Japan halted live cattle exports, adding late female genetics to the North American fullblood population. Individual pedigree records were not found in the sources reviewed. She is listed as a verifiable named foundation female from the 1997 import.
notes: Named in 1997 Mannett import list; no individual pedigree found.

name: Okuito 9
bloodline_detail: Part of the Mannett Group's 1997 black female import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Okuito 9 was one of seven black foundation females in the Mannett Group's 1997 import from Japan (with the bull Yasufuku Jr), the last World K's shipment before the export ban. Alongside Taguchi 9, Nakahana 5, Mitsutaka, Hanateru 9, Rabito and Hisako she contributed additional maternal lines to the fullblood herd outside Japan. Her individual pedigree is not documented in the sources reviewed. She is included as a verifiable named foundation female from the final Mannett consignment.
notes: Named in 1997 Mannett import list; no individual pedigree found.

name: Hanateru 9
bloodline_detail: Part of the Mannett Group's 1997 black female import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Hanateru 9 was one of the seven black foundation females imported from Japan by the Mannett Group in 1997, arriving with the bull Yasufuku Jr in the last World K's consignment before Japan closed exports. She joined Taguchi 9, Nakahana 5, Mitsutaka, Okuito 9, Rabito and Hisako in broadening the fullblood female base abroad. Individual pedigree detail is not published in the sources reviewed. She is included as a verifiable named foundation female of the 1997 import.
notes: Named in 1997 Mannett import list; no individual pedigree found.

name: Rabito
bloodline_detail: Part of the Mannett Group's 1997 black female import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Rabito was one of seven black foundation females imported from Japan by the Mannett Group in 1997, the final World K's shipment (which also brought the bull Yasufuku Jr) before the Japanese export ban. Together with the rest of her cohort she added late maternal genetics to the North American fullblood herd. Her individual pedigree was not found in the sources reviewed. She is listed as a verifiable named foundation female from the 1997 import.
notes: Named in 1997 Mannett import list; no individual pedigree found.

name: Hisako
bloodline_detail: Part of the Mannett Group's 1997 black female import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Hisako was one of the seven black foundation females in the Mannett Group's 1997 import from Japan, arriving with the bull Yasufuku Jr in the last World K's consignment before live exports ceased. With Taguchi 9, Nakahana 5, Mitsutaka, Okuito 9, Hanateru 9 and Rabito, she rounded out the final infusion of Japan-exported female genetics into the North American fullblood herd. Her pedigree is not documented in the sources reviewed. She is included as a verifiable named foundation female of the 1997 import.
notes: Named in 1997 Mannett import list; no individual pedigree found.

name: Chisahime 662
bloodline_detail: One of the black foundation females imported by Japanese Venture Partners (JVP) in 1994. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Chisahime 662 was one of the group of black foundation females imported from Japan by Japanese Venture Partners (JVP) in 1994 — the same consignment that brought the influential Tajima bulls Fukutsuru 068, Kikuyasu-400 and Yasutanisakura. JVP's import added an important block of maternal genetics to the young North American fullblood herd alongside the Mannett and Takeda groups. Individual pedigree, registration and birth-year records for Chisahime 662 are not published in the accessible sources, so those fields are left blank. She is documented as one of the verifiable Japan-exported JVP foundation dams.
notes: JVP 1994 foundation female per Wagyu International; individual pedigree not found.

name: Chiyofuku 992
bloodline_detail: Black foundation female of the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners (JVP) import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Chiyofuku 992 was among the black foundation females imported by Japanese Venture Partners in 1994, the shipment led by the Tajima bulls Fukutsuru 068 and Kikuyasu-400. As one of the JVP dams she contributed to the maternal foundation of the fullblood herd outside Japan. Her individual pedigree and registration are not documented in the accessible sources and are left blank. She is included as a verifiable named JVP foundation female.
notes: JVP 1994 foundation female per Wagyu International; individual pedigree not found.

name: Fukutomi 990
bloodline_detail: Black foundation female of the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners (JVP) import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Fukutomi 990 was one of the black foundation females in the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners importation, the consignment that also brought the celebrated Tajima marbling sire Fukutsuru 068. She adds to the small, documented set of JVP foundation dams behind the fullblood herd outside Japan. Her individual pedigree and registration are not published in the accessible sources. She is listed as a verifiable JVP foundation female.
notes: JVP 1994 foundation female per Wagyu International; individual pedigree not found.

name: Kikuhana 298
bloodline_detail: Black foundation female of the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners (JVP) import. Distinct from the Takeda BULL named Kikuhana (TF37).
bio: Kikuhana 298 was a black foundation female imported by Japanese Venture Partners in 1994, alongside the Tajima bulls Fukutsuru 068, Kikuyasu-400 and Yasutanisakura. She should not be confused with the Takeda-imported bull also named Kikuhana (tag TF37) — a different animal of a different sex. Her individual pedigree and registration are not documented in the accessible sources. She is a verifiable named JVP foundation dam.
notes: JVP 1994 foundation female per Wagyu International; disambiguated from bull Kikuhana TF37.

name: Shigehime 208
bloodline_detail: Black foundation female of the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners (JVP) import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Shigehime 208 was among the black foundation females of the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners importation. As one of the JVP dams she helped broaden the maternal base of the North American fullblood herd during the pivotal 1990s import window. Her individual pedigree and registration are not published in the accessible sources and are left blank. She is included as a verifiable named JVP foundation female.
notes: JVP 1994 foundation female per Wagyu International; individual pedigree not found.

name: Tokuhime 486
bloodline_detail: Black foundation female of the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners (JVP) import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Tokuhime 486 was one of the black foundation females imported by Japanese Venture Partners in 1994. She belongs to the documented JVP group of dams that, with the Mannett and Takeda females, made up the maternal foundation of fullblood Wagyu outside Japan. Her individual pedigree and registration are not documented in the accessible sources. She is a verifiable named JVP foundation dam.
notes: JVP 1994 foundation female per Wagyu International; individual pedigree not found.

name: Yasufuji 1/4
bloodline_detail: Black foundation female of the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners (JVP) import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Yasufuji 1/4 was a black foundation female in the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners importation, the shipment led by the Tajima bulls Fukutsuru 068 and Kikuyasu-400. As a JVP dam she contributed to the maternal genetics of the early fullblood herd outside Japan. Her individual pedigree and registration are not published in the accessible sources. She is included as a verifiable named JVP foundation female.
notes: JVP 1994 foundation female per Wagyu International; individual pedigree not found.

name: Yoshifuku 2
bloodline_detail: Black foundation female of the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners (JVP) import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Yoshifuku 2 was among the black foundation females of the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners importation. One of the JVP dams, she is part of the documented maternal foundation of fullblood Wagyu outside Japan. Her individual pedigree and registration are not published in the accessible sources and are left blank. She is a verifiable named JVP foundation dam.
notes: JVP 1994 foundation female per Wagyu International; individual pedigree not found.

She rounds out the JVP block of dams that, alongside the Mannett and Takeda groups, formed the maternal base of fullblood Wagyu outside Japan. Her individual pedigree and registration are not documented in the accessible sources. She is a verifiable named JVP foundation dam.

name: Kunisakae
bloodline_detail: Foundation heifer of the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners (JVP) import. Individual strain composition not documented.
bio: Kunisakae was one of the foundation heifers in the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners importation, a young female shipped alongside the JVP bulls and cows. As part of that documented group she contributed to the fullblood maternal foundation outside Japan. Her individual pedigree, registration and birth year are not published in the accessible sources. She is included as a verifiable named JVP foundation heifer.
notes: JVP 1994 foundation heifer per Wagyu International; individual detail not found.

name: 27 Homare
bloodline_detail: Red (Akaushi) foundation heifer of the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners (JVP) import — one of the few red females JVP brought in.
bio: 27 Homare was a red Akaushi foundation heifer in the 1994 Japanese Venture Partners importation — notable because JVP's shipment was predominantly black, so its handful of red females added to the small pool of Akaushi genetics that came out of Japan beyond the Wood/HeartBrand group. Her individual pedigree, registration and birth year are not documented in the accessible sources. She is included as a verifiable named JVP foundation Akaushi heifer.
notes: JVP 1994 red foundation heifer per Wagyu International; individual detail not found.
